hero.tagline: Standard $6,000 cap, 14-day appeal window, and broad eligibility for most money claims.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Court
at_a_glance.value: Circuit Court District Division (small claims docket)

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in Superior Court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $6,000, you must either waive the excess to proceed in small claims or file in a higher court. You cannot split a single dispute into multiple small claims to circumvent the cap.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: New Hampshire allows most contract and debt claims up to $6,000 in small claims court. The statute of limitations is generally 3 years for written and oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A roofer signed a $5,000 contract to replace your roof, took a $2,000 deposit, and never started the work.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the breach to file. Bring the signed contract, payment records, and any communications.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to pay a neighbor $500 to paint your fence, but they never showed up after you paid.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Bring proof of payment and any witnesses to the agreement.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $2,000 with the promise of repayment in 6 months, but they never paid you back.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from when repayment was due. Bring evidence of the loan and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Unpaid promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a $3,000 promissory note and defaulted on payments.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from default. Bring the original note and payment history.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for goods/services
claims.example: You provided $1,200 in landscaping services and the client never paid your invoice.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from when payment was due. Bring the invoice and proof of service.

claims.name: Open account or credit card debt
claims.example: A customer owes $4,500 on a store credit account and stopped making payments.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from last payment or charge. Bring account statements and payment records.

claims.name: Unpaid rent (money only)
claims.example: A tenant owes $2,000 in back rent after moving out.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from when rent was due. Bring the lease and payment records.

claims.name: Bad check (NSF or closed account)
claims.example: A customer paid you $800 with a check that bounced.
claims.notes: You must give a 14-day written notice before suing. Bring the returned check and notice proof.
claims.damage_boost: You may recover the check amount plus a statutory penalty.

claims.name: Co-signer liability for unpaid debt
claims.example: You co-signed a $3,000 loan and the primary borrower defaulted.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from default. Bring the loan agreement and payment records.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Small claims court covers most consumer disputes up to $6,000, including defective products and unfair practices. The Consumer Protection Act may provide extra remedies.

claims.name: Defective product (not as advertised)
claims.example: You bought a $1,000 appliance that never worked and the seller refused a refund.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring receipts, product documentation, and correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Treble damages and attorney fees may be available for willful violations.

claims.name: Bad service (not as promised)
claims.example: A cleaning company charged $400 but did not perform the agreed services.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring service agreement, payment proof, and photos if applicable.

claims.name: Auto repair dispute
claims.example: A mechanic charged $1,200 for repairs that did not fix the problem.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring repair order, receipts, and any expert opinions.

claims.name: Home contractor dispute
claims.example: A contractor took a $2,500 deposit and never started the job.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring contract, payment proof, and communications.

claims.name: No-show vendor
claims.example: A DJ took a $600 deposit for a wedding and did not show up.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring contract, payment proof, and event documentation.

claims.name: Subscription cancellation dispute
claims.example: A gym refused to cancel your $300 membership after you followed their process.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring contract, cancellation request, and payment records.

claims.name: Used car sale dispute
claims.example: You bought a $5,000 used car that broke down within a week and the dealer refused repairs.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring bill of sale, repair records, and correspondence.

claims.name: False advertising
claims.example: A store advertised a laptop for $800 but refused to honor the price.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring ad copy, purchase attempt records, and correspondence.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Claims for property damage up to $6,000 are allowed. The statute of limitations is generally 3 years.

claims.name: Vehicle collision (property only)
claims.example: Someone rear-ended your car, causing $2,500 in damage, and refused to pay.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring repair estimates, police report, and photos.

claims.name: Neighbor damages property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell and damaged your $1,800 fence.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring photos, repair estimates, and any communications.

claims.name: Damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant caused $1,200 in damage to your apartment.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring lease, photos, and repair bills.

claims.name: Pet damages property
claims.example: A dog chewed up your $600 garden furniture.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring photos and repair/replacement receipts.

claims.name: Property damaged while in custody
claims.example: A storage facility lost or damaged your $2,000 belongings.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring storage contract, inventory, and damage proof.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant (money only)
claim_categories.blurb: Small claims court covers money disputes between landlords and tenants, such as security deposits and unpaid rent. Evictions are excluded.

claims.name: Security deposit not returned
claims.example: A landlord kept your $1,000 deposit without explanation after you moved out.
claims.notes: Landlord must return deposit or itemize deductions within 30 days. Bring lease, move-out photos, and correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Tenant may recover double the amount wrongfully withheld.

claims.name: Deposit deductions not itemized
claims.example: Landlord deducted $400 from your deposit but gave no itemized list.
claims.notes: Landlord must provide itemized statement within 30 days. Bring lease and move-out documentation.

claims.name: Uninhabitable rental (money only)
claims.example: You paid $1,200 for a month in an apartment with no heat or water.
claims.notes: Bring lease, photos, and repair requests. Only money damages, not repairs or injunctions.

claims.name: Landlord keeps tenant property
claims.example: Landlord kept your $800 bicycle after you moved out.
claims.notes: Bring lease, move-out documentation, and proof of property value.

claims.name: Illegal eviction or lockout (money only)
claims.example: Landlord changed the locks and kept your $1,000 rent.
claims.notes: Bring lease, police report, and proof of damages. Only money damages.

claims.name: Unreturned prepaid rent
claims.example: Landlord kept your $600 prepaid rent after early termination.
claims.notes: Bring lease, payment records, and move-out documentation.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Tenant owes $2,000 in back rent after moving out.
claims.notes: Bring lease and payment records. Only money damages.

claims.name: Repair and deduct dispute
claims.example: Tenant paid $300 to fix a broken heater and landlord refused reimbursement.
claims.notes: Bring repair receipts and notice to landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wages
claim_categories.blurb: Small claims court covers most wage and employment disputes up to $6,000. The statute of limitations is generally 3 years.

claims.name: Unpaid wages
claims.example: Employer owes you $1,000 for hours worked but not paid.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring pay stubs, timesheets, and employment agreement.

claims.name: Final paycheck not paid
claims.example: Employer failed to pay your $800 final paycheck after termination.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring termination letter and pay records.

claims.name: Unpaid commissions
claims.example: Salesperson owed $2,500 in commissions after leaving job.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring commission agreement and sales records.

claims.name: Unpaid overtime
claims.example: Employer did not pay $600 in overtime wages.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring timesheets and pay records.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Small claims court covers minor personal claims for money, such as loaned property or civil theft, up to $6,000.

claims.name: Minor personal injury (money only)
claims.example: You slipped on a neighbor's icy walkway and incurred $1,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring medical bills and incident documentation.

claims.name: Loaned property not returned
claims.example: You loaned a $400 power tool to a friend who never gave it back.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring proof of ownership and loan.

claims.name: Civil theft (money damages only)
claims.example: Someone stole your $1,200 bicycle and you seek its value.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring police report and proof of value.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Vehicle-related claims such as mechanic disputes and wrongful tows are allowed up to $6,000.

claims.name: Mechanic/auto repair dispute
claims.example: Mechanic charged $900 for repairs that did not fix your car.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring repair order, receipts, and expert opinion if possible.

claims.name: Wrongful tow
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal spot and you paid $300 to recover it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline. Bring tow receipt, photos, and correspondence.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction actions are not allowed in small claims court; only money damages can be sought.
Small claims court cannot decide ownership or title to real property.
Claims for libel or slander are excluded from small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims are not allowed in small claims court.
Divorce, child custody, and probate matters are excluded.
Bankruptcy, patents, trademarks, and other federal claims are not allowed.
Class actions are not permitted in small claims court.
Small claims court cannot order someone to do or stop doing something.
Workers' compensation claims are handled by the Department of Labor.
Claims against deceased persons must be filed in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not allowed, but statutory penalties and multipliers may apply for certain claims such as bad checks or consumer fraud.

statutory_multipliers.multiplier: 2x check amount or $100, whichever is greater
statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount plus a civil penalty of either $100 or twice the check amount, whichever is greater, after proper notice.
Tenant may recover double the amount wrongfully withheld if landlord fails to comply with deposit return rules.
Court may award up to three times actual damages for willful or knowing violations.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be awarded for Consumer Protection Act claims and certain statutory violations, but not for ordinary contract or debt claims.
damages.postjudgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 2% per year on judgments.

filing_options: Any county where ONE defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: The court may transfer the case to the correct venue or dismiss it; you may need to refile and pay a new fee.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing is not currently available for small claims.

eligibility_bullets: Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: Waiver covers filing fee and may cover service fees if granted.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: Waived fees may be added to the judgment against the losing party.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, the filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud, personal injury, and property damage claims.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or bankruptcy is filed.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: A demand letter is not required for most claims but is recommended. For bad checks, a 14-day written notice is mandatory before filing.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: You must give the check writer a 14-day written notice to pay before suing for a bad check.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific written demand required, but recommended to document your request for unpaid wages.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Tenants must provide a forwarding address for deposit return. Landlords must itemize deductions within 30 days.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No special pre-filing notice required for Consumer Protection Act claims.

forms.name: Small Claim Complaint
forms.description: Used to start a small claims case by stating your claim and the amount sought.

forms.name: Small Claim Answer
forms.description: Used by the defendant to respond to a small claim.

forms.name: Motion to Waive Filing Fee
forms.description: Request to waive the filing fee due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Small Claim Judgment
forms.description: Form used by the court to record the outcome of the case.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: Court clerk sends the papers by certified mail to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent for corporations/LLCs; serve the owner for sole proprietors.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Long-arm jurisdiction applies if the cause of action arose in New Hampshire.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be found, you may request permission for substitute service or publication.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is dodging service, you may use a private process server or request alternate service from the court.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendant is not required to file a written answer but may do so. Must appear at the hearing or risk default.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant does not appear, the court may enter a default judgment for the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Plaintiff must still prove damages even if the defendant defaults.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, you may appeal within 14 days.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by an attorney but are not required to have one.
hearing.format: Bench trial before a judge; hearings are typically brief and informal.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request in advance through the court clerk.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts offer mediation on the day of hearing.

appeals.type_notes: The case is heard anew in Superior Court.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment cannot be appealed directly; you must first file a motion to vacate.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 2% per year on unpaid judgments.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of execution with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of wages and remits to the court until the judgment is satisfied.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective if the debtor is steadily employed.

methods.name: Bank account levy
methods.blurb: Freeze and seize funds from debtor's bank account.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and pays out available funds up to the judgment amount.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if you know where the debtor banks.

methods.name: Lien on real property
methods.blurb: Record a lien against debtor's real estate.
methods.description: Record the judgment with the county registry of deeds to create a lien on any real property owned by the debtor.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real estate; paid when property is sold or refinanced.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew before expiration; use NHJB-2210-DP.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Automatic stay applies; most judgments for fraud, willful injury, or certain debts may survive bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: First to record the lien has priority among judgment creditors.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses may appear without an attorney in small claims court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Must use the legal entity name, not just a trade name.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may not be able to recover fees for unlicensed work.

pitfalls.title: Wrong defendant naming
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue the wrong legal entity, your judgment may be unenforceable.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Check Secretary of State records and use the exact legal name.

pitfalls.title: Missed statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the deadline results in dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Check the SOL for your claim type before filing.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient proof of damages
pitfalls.what_happens: You may win liability but get only nominal damages.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Bring receipts, estimates, and photos to court.

pitfalls.title: Improper service
pitfalls.what_happens: Case may be dismissed or delayed if service is not done correctly.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow court rules for service and file proof promptly.

pitfalls.title: Suing for more than $6,000
pitfalls.what_happens: You must waive the excess or file in a higher court.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Limit your claim to $6,000 or pursue the full amount elsewhere.

pitfalls.title: Not bringing all evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: The judge may rule against you if you lack documentation.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Prepare all relevant documents and witnesses before the hearing.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a lawyer in New Hampshire small claims court. Parties may represent themselves, and lawyers are allowed but not required. Many people handle their own cases.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Most small claims cases are resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing to judgment, depending on court scheduling and whether the defendant appears.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can claim in New Hampshire small claims court is $6,000, not including interest and court costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment for the plaintiff, but the plaintiff must still prove their damages.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Either party can appeal a small claims judgment within 14 days by filing in Superior Court. The appeal is a new trial (trial de novo).

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A small claims judgment in New Hampshire is valid for 20 years and can be renewed before expiration.

state_specific_notes: New Hampshire small claims court allows both individuals and businesses to file up to $6,000.
Double damages are available for wrongfully withheld security deposits.
Treble damages and attorney fees may be awarded for willful Consumer Protection Act violations.
A 14-day written notice is required before suing for a bad check.
No e-filing is available for small claims as of 2026.
Evictions and title to real estate are excluded from small claims.
You must use the legal entity name when suing a business.
Default judgments require proof of damages even if the defendant does not appear.
Some courts offer mediation on the day of hearing.

interesting_findings: New Hampshire's small claims process is relatively informal and designed for self-represented litigants.
The court may offer mediation services at no extra cost.
Filing fees are tiered by claim amount, making it more affordable for smaller claims.
Judgments can be enforced for up to 20 years, which is longer than many states.
The Consumer Protection Act provides strong remedies for consumer fraud, including treble damages.
